17: 'A razor has never come upon my head, for I have been a Nazirite to God from my mother's womb.' Samson's final disclosure of the secret of his strength identifies it as Nazirite consecration — a set-apart life from birth. The parallel to Christ is the ultimate consecration: 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people... As he spoke by the mouth of his holy prophets from of old... to show mercy to our fathers and to remember his holy covenant' (Luke 1:68-72). Christ is the one holy and consecrated from eternity (Ephesians 1:4 — 'chosen in him before the foundation of the world'). Where Samson's consecration is violated and the source of strength removed, Christ's holiness is unbreakable — he who 'knew no sin' (2 Corinthians 5:21) could not be defiled and could not be held by death.
20: 'He did not know that YHWH had left him.' The most tragic sentence in Judges — and a type of the most anguished cry in the NT: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46, citing Psalms 22:1). Where Samson's abandonment is the consequence of covenant violation (his Nazirite vow broken), Christ's dereliction is the consequence of substitutionary atonement — he who knew no sin becomes sin (2 Corinthians 5:21), experiencing the full weight of divine abandonment that sinners deserve. Samson did not know YHWH had left; Christ cries it from the cross, fully conscious of the abandonment he is undergoing on behalf of others. The type is partial — the antitype is total and willing.
28: Samson's final prayer — 'Remember me, O Lord YHWH, and please strengthen me just this once, O God, that I may be avenged' — is a lament prayer from the depths: blind, enslaved, mocked. The element of 'remembering' (zākar) is the covenant memory-language: when YHWH 'remembers,' he acts on prior commitment (Genesis 8:1 — God remembered Noah; Exodus 2:24 — God remembered his covenant with Abraham). The thief on the cross echoes this exact form: 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom' (Luke 23:42). Both are at the point of death, disgraced, asking only to be remembered by the covenant God. Both prayers are heard.
30: 'He killed more at his death than he had killed during his life.' The final Samson verdict is the pattern of Christ's cross in miniature: 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (John 12:24). The living Samson killed Philistines one by one; the dying Samson kills 3,000 in a moment. The living Jesus gathered twelve disciples; the dying Jesus draws all people to himself (John 12:32 — 'And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself'). The cross produces more fruit than the entire earthly ministry — Pentecost (3,000 in a day, Acts 2:41) follows Calvary. The Samson pattern is the seed of the gospel principle: death is more fruitful than life when it is a death of consecrated self-giving.
1: Samson goes to Gaza and sees a prostitute there and goes in to her. Gaza is the principal Philistine city — the deep enemy heartland. Samson's entry into Gaza repeats the pattern of Judges 14: the Spirit-empowered deliverer goes into the enemy's territory and is drawn by a woman there. The progression is downward: first a Philistine wife (Judges 14), then a prostitute in Gaza (Judges 16:1), then Delilah (Judges 16:4). Each transgression represents a deeper entanglement with the Philistine world. The Christological point of contrast: where Samson's passion for the wrong women draws him progressively deeper into enemy territory and eventual capture, Christ enters the enemy's territory (John 12:31: 'the ruler of this world') deliberately and sovereignly, not driven by passion but by the Father's love (John 3:16), and he exits by resurrection rather than betrayal.
2: The Gazites surround Samson and wait all night at the city gate to kill him in the morning: 'When it is morning, we will kill him.' The ambush planned for morning is the enemy's strategy against the anointed one: wait for the appointed time, then strike. The same pattern governs the Passion: the chief priests plan to arrest Jesus quietly, wait for the right moment (Luke 22:2-6), and hand him over at dawn for crucifixion. But the morning that the enemies of YHWH's anointed plan for death becomes the morning of resurrection — the tomb is found empty at dawn on the first day (Mark 16:2; John 20:1). The Gazites' plan to kill at morning is undone by Samson's midnight escape; the Sanhedrin's plan for dawn execution is undone by the empty tomb.
3: Samson lies until midnight, then rises and takes the city gate posts — doors, two gateposts, bar and all — and puts them on his shoulders and carries them to the top of a hill near Hebron. The city gate was both the city's primary defense and its judicial center; to carry away the gate is to remove both the protection of the enemy city and the seat of its power. The typological significance is rich: Christ's harrowing of hell (1 Peter 3:18-20; Matthew 16:18 — 'the gates of hell shall not prevail') and resurrection are figured as a carrying-away of death's gate. The city of the dead cannot hold the anointed one; he exits bearing the very mechanism of his containment on his shoulders. Isaiah 9:6 speaks of 'the government upon his shoulder' — here the government (the gate of the city) is carried on the deliverer's shoulders as a trophy of victory.
4: After this Samson loves a woman in the Valley of Sorek whose name is Delilah. The name Delilah possibly derives from dallāh (to weaken/make low) — a name encoding the narrative's theological function: she is the one through whom the strong is brought low. The Valley of Sorek is grape-country on the border of Philistia; its name (śōrēq, choice vine) evokes the vineyard imagery of Israel's covenant identity (Isaiah 5:1-7; Psalms 80:8-9). The choice vine becomes the site of Samson's final vulnerability — love in the vineyard becoming the mechanism of betrayal, as the garden of Gethsemane becomes the site of Judas's betrayal of Jesus (Matthew 26:36-47).
5: The five Philistine lords offer Delilah each 1100 pieces of silver to discover the source of Samson's strength. Five lords at 1100 pieces each totals 5500 pieces — the value placed on Samson's betrayal. The specificity of the price echoes the value placed on the betrayal of Christ (thirty pieces of silver, Matthew 26:15) — both are the precise negotiated cost of handing over an anointed deliverer to his enemies. The use of a woman as the mechanism of betrayal for money is the amplified form of the Judges 14 pattern: the same dynamic of intimate betrayal for financial reward that prefigured Judas is here developed to its fullest expression before the final catastrophic betrayal of Judges 16.
6: Delilah asks Samson directly: 'Please tell me where your great strength lies, and how you might be bound, that one could subdue you.' The directness of the question — Delilah does not disguise her agenda — is part of the narrative's dark comedy: Samson lies to her three times yet keeps returning. The explicit request for the secret of his strength and the means of his binding is the enemy's abiding question about the source of the anointed one's power. The rulers who crucified Christ also misunderstood the source of his power and thought the cross would eliminate it; they did not know that his power flows from his relationship with the Father and that death would amplify rather than end it (John 10:17-18).
7: Samson gives his first false answer: seven fresh bowstrings not yet dried. The number seven in the context of Samson's Nazirite calling is significant: his seven locks (v19) are bound up with his consecration; here he proposes seven bowstrings as a false surrogate for the true source of his power. The number seven recurs across the three lies (seven bowstrings, new ropes, weaving of hair), marking each false answer as a parody of completeness. The enemy seeking the source of the anointed one's power consistently receives incomplete answers — 'none of the rulers of this age understood this, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory' (1 Corinthians 2:8).
8: The Philistine lords bring Delilah the seven fresh bowstrings, and she binds Samson with them while men are lying in wait. The ambush men hidden in the inner room waiting for Delilah's signal establish the covert coordination between Delilah and the Philistine intelligence network — Samson's intimacy is being surveilled. The hidden watchers within the house of love are a type of the hidden forces that coordinated the Passion: 'Judas knew the place' (John 18:2), and the coordination was arranged in advance, with a prearranged signal (a kiss, Matthew 26:48). The betrayal of the anointed one requires insider knowledge and coordinated action; it cannot happen by mere external force.
9: With the men waiting in ambush, Delilah cried to him, ‘The Philistines are upon you, Samson!’ But he snapped the bowstrings as a strand of tow snaps when it touches fire. So the secret of his strength was not known. The first failed test establishes the narrative pattern that will repeat three times. Samson's effortless breaking of the bowstrings when the alarm is raised demonstrates that the source of his strength is not in any material object but in his relationship with YHWH. The same logic governs the NT's understanding of Christ's power: no external binding, no tomb, no seal can contain the power of the resurrection (Acts 2:24: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it').
10: Delilah accuses Samson: 'You have mocked me and told me lies. Please tell me how you might be bound.' The accusation of mockery and lies — hetel ʾōtî (you have mocked/deceived me) — is legitimate from Delilah's perspective but covenantally invalid: Delilah is the enemy's agent, and Samson's deception of her is not a violation of covenant fidelity. The Christological parallel is the charge of deception brought against Christ: 'Sir, we remember how that impostor said' (Matthew 27:63). The enemies who seek to bind the anointed one characterize his protection of the divine mystery as deception — because they cannot comprehend that truth has a right to protect itself from those who would weaponize it.
11: Samson offers his second lie: new ropes never used. The progression from bowstrings (weapon-equipment) to new ropes (general-purpose binding) escalates the apparent plausibility while maintaining the fiction. The new ropes recall Samson's voluntary submission to binding by the men of Judah in Judges 15:13; there the ropes melted like flax before him when the Spirit came. The ropes that could not hold him in covenant solidarity with Israel certainly cannot hold him in Philistine captivity. The anointed one cannot be held by the bonds his enemies devise — only by the bonds he himself accepts.
12: Delilah binds Samson with new ropes while men lie in wait in the inner room; she cries 'The Philistines are upon you, Samson!' He breaks the ropes from his arms like a thread. The second failed test confirms the pattern: external bonds cannot contain Samson's Spirit-empowered strength. The tearing of ropes 'like a thread' echoes the great-strength tradition of Israel's heroes (Acts 16:26, where an earthquake breaks the stocks holding Paul and Silas). The liberation from bonds by divine power is the recurring sign of YHWH's championship of his anointed — from Joseph released from prison (Genesis 41:14) to Peter released from chains (Acts 12:7) to Christ rising from the sealed tomb (Matthew 28:2-6).
13: Samson gives his third lie: weave the seven locks of his hair into the web of a loom. This answer is closer to the truth than the previous two — the hair is mentioned, and the number seven reappears. The narrowing of the lies toward the truth is a narrative pressure that mirrors the escalating intimacy of the betrayal: each false answer is closer to the secret, each test more invasive. Delilah weaves his hair while he sleeps, and the peg is driven in — a foreshadowing of v19 where the actual shaving occurs during sleep. The sleeping vulnerable man in the lap of the woman who will betray him is the Passion picture in outline.
14: She pins it with the peg and cries 'The Philistines are upon you!' He wakes and goes away with the peg of the loom and the web — the third test fails. Samson waking with the loom's peg trailing from his hair is almost comic: the man who cannot be bound is leaving with his would-be binder attached. The absurdity of the successive failed attempts signals the narrative's theological point: the strength of the Spirit-anointed one is invulnerable to every external mechanism of control — bowstrings, ropes, even a woven bond attached to the very source of his strength. Only the voluntary disclosure of the secret can transfer the power; only Samson's own speech will deliver him.
15: Delilah presses: ‘How can you say you love me when your heart is not with me? You have mocked me these three times.’ The accusation ‘you do not love me’ is the emotional leverage that will finally break Samson’s resistance (v16-17). The appeal to love as the ground of disclosure — ‘if you loved me you would tell me’ — is the perversion of covenant intimacy: love is being weaponized to extract confession. The Gethsemane pattern reverses this: Christ does not need to extract confession from the Father because their love is perfect; he discloses himself fully not under emotional pressure but in the freedom of love (‘I lay down my life of my own accord,’ John 10:18).
16: When she pressed him hard with her words day after day, urging him, his soul was vexed to death (wattāṣar napšô lāmût). The phrase 'his soul was vexed to death' is the same Hebrew construction as the Gethsemane agony: 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death' (perilypos estin hē psychē mou heōs thanatou, Matthew 26:38). In both cases the anointed one's soul is pressed to the point of death by the one closest to him — Delilah's sustained pestering and the disciples' failure to watch with Jesus are both intimate failures that precede surrender to the enemy. Samson's soul pressed to death by Delilah's words; Christ's soul pressed to death in Gethsemane under the weight of human sin.
18: Delilah sends for the Philistine lords and they come with the money in their hands — the coins present at the moment of betrayal. 'She made him sleep on her knees, and called for a man and had him shave off the seven locks of his head.' The image of Samson asleep on Delilah's knees while the shaver removes his consecration is one of the OT's most charged moments of vulnerability: the deliverer stripped of his Nazirite sign in a state of trusting repose. The type of Christ is the sleeping in the garden: 'could you not watch with me one hour?' (Matthew 26:40). In both cases the anointed one's rest in the place of apparent safety becomes the moment of his handing over.
19: She shaved off his seven locks; his strength left him (wayyāsar kōḥô mēʿālāyw). The departure of strength with the removal of the seven locks is the departure of the Spirit's empowerment — not because hair is intrinsically magical but because the Nazirite vow's integrity was the vessel of Samson's covenant relationship with YHWH, and its violation was the violation of that relationship. The seven locks shaved echoes the Nazirite legislation of Numbers 6:18 where the Nazirite's hair is shaved at the fulfillment of the vow — here it is shaved in betrayal rather than completion. The stripping of Samson's consecration is the typological precursor of Christ's stripping at the cross (Matthew 27:28, 35) — the one who is fully consecrated to YHWH is stripped of every external mark of dignity by his enemies.
21: The Philistines seize Samson, gouge out his eyes, bring him down to Gaza, bind him with bronze shackles, and he grinds grain in prison. Three acts of humiliation: blindness, binding, and servile labor. The blinding of the one who had seen the Timnite woman and Delilah — whose eyes had driven him to transgression — is the poetic justice of covenant discipline: the organ of his sin becomes the site of his punishment. The grinding of grain in prison is slave-woman's work (Exodus 11:5; Isaiah 47:2). Christ's humiliation includes being bound (John 18:12), stripped, and mocked (Matthew 27:27-31) — the anointed one reduced to the condition of a slave in fulfillment of Isaiah 53:3-5. The bronze shackles that bind Samson echo the bronze chains of Zedekiah (2 Kings 25:7) — two anointed leaders bound in bronze after covenant failure.
22: 'But the hair of his head began to grow again after it had been shaved.' The single verse of hopeful reversal embedded in Samson's nadir is the narrative's resurrection announcement: the sign of consecration is returning. The growing of hair is biologically inevitable — it cannot be permanently removed. The type of resurrection is embedded in the structure of biology: cut off, it grows back; the Nazirite consecration cannot be permanently nullified. Christ's death has the same biological-theological structure: 'it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:24). The hair growing in the dark of the prison is the resurrection seed germinating before any public announcement.
23: The Philistine lords assemble to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon their god and to rejoice: 'Our god has given Samson our enemy into our hand.' The victory celebration over the captured deliverer is the pattern repeated at the cross: 'He saved others; he cannot save himself' (Matthew 27:42); the mocking at Golgotha parallels the Philistine celebration — the apparent victory of the enemy over the anointed one. The specific attribution of Samson's capture to Dagon ('our god has given him into our hand') is the enemy's theological claim that their deity has defeated YHWH's judge. The premature victory celebration is invariably the sign of imminent overthrow: ‘while people are saying there is peace and security, then sudden destruction will come upon them’ (1 Thessalonians 5:3).
24: When the people see Samson, they praise their god: 'Our god has given our enemy into our hand, the ravager of our country who has killed many of us.' The corporate praise of Dagon for Samson's capture is the mirror image of Israel's victory songs — the same liturgical structure (see, praise, attribution to deity) deployed for the enemy's celebration. The theological reversal the narrative is building toward is that YHWH will receive the praise after Dagon's house falls: the praise currently given to Dagon will be silenced under the rubble of his own temple. The enemies of the anointed one who prematurely celebrate ('Let him come down from the cross and we will believe in him,' Matthew 27:42) find their celebration turned to terror at the resurrection.
25: When their hearts are merry they call for Samson to be brought out to perform — wᵉyᵉśaḥeq lānû, 'that he may entertain us.' The word śāḥaq (laugh/sport/play) is the same root as Isaac's name — the one given as the instrument of YHWH's laughter at the enemies (Psalms 2:4). Samson the judge is made to perform as a court jester before the Philistine lords. The humiliation of Christ before Herod's court — 'Herod with his soldiers treated him with contempt and mocked him' (Luke 23:11) — and before the Roman soldiers (Matthew 27:27-31) is the NT fulfillment: the anointed one made to perform for his captors. Both Samson and Christ endure public mockery orchestrated by the enemy before the decisive act that ends the enemy's power.
26: Samson asks the young man who holds his hand to let him touch the pillars that support the house. Samson's blindness means he must be guided to the place of his final act — yet his request shows intentionality and planning: he knows what he will do when he reaches the pillars. The blind anointed one guided to the place of his final act is a type of Christ's Gethsemane prayer — knowing full well what is coming, walking deliberately toward it ('knowing all that would happen to him,' John 18:4). The anointed one is not carried to the site of his death; he goes to it with knowledge and purpose, submitting to being led only because the journey requires it.
27: The house was full of men and women; all the Philistine lords were there, and about three thousand men and women on the roof watching. The assembly of Philistine power — lords, thousands, the entire leadership watching the sport — concentrates all the forces that have oppressed Israel into one structure above Samson's head. The 'about three thousand' on the roof recalls the three thousand who die at Sinai's golden calf (Exodus 32:28) and the three thousand saved at Pentecost (Acts 2:41) — the same number marking covenant judgment and covenant inauguration. The crowd assembled to watch the anointed one's final performance is the same pattern as the crowd at the cross (Matthew 27:39-44) — the public, mocking audience at the event that will transform history.
29: Samson grasps the two middle pillars on which the house rested — he leans his right hand on one and his left hand on the other. The posture of Samson with arms extended to both sides — right hand on one pillar, left hand on the other — is the cruciform posture of outstretched arms. The image of the anointed one with arms spread between two supports, with all the powers of the enemy arrayed above him, is one of the OT's most direct typological images of the cross. The two pillars flanking the central space may correspond to the two thieves flanking Christ at Golgotha (Matthew 27:38). The structure Samson destroys is the house of Dagon — a false god whose worship will be ended by the very act of the anointed one's death.
31: Samson's brothers and his father's whole family come down, take his body, and bury him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of his father Manoah. The burial by family members in the ancestral tomb is the honorable burial accorded to a judge who fulfilled his calling. The Christological contrast: where Samson is buried in his father's tomb — the ancestral sepulcher of his family — Christ is buried in a borrowed tomb belonging to Joseph of Arimathea (Matthew 27:60; Isaiah 53:9: 'they made his grave with the rich'). Both are accorded honorable burial by those who loved them; both burials are temporary. The note that Samson 'judged Israel twenty years' is the judge's formal legacy — Christ's legacy is 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18): not twenty years but forever.
